skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion component code into MP4 videos using the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.summary: Turn Remotion TSX components into configurable MP4 video renders.
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell and URL patterns, but the Markdown mostly documents expected belt CLI usage and reference links. The confirmed risk is that user-provided TSX code and props are sent to the external inference.sh rendering service, so users need a clear third-party data disclosure warning. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or weak cryptographic code.

medium_findings.title: User Code Sent to External Rendering Service
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to send TSX component code and props to infsh/remotion-render through the belt CLI. This is expected behavior for remote rendering, but proprietary code, private data, or embedded secrets could be disclosed to inference.sh.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly pass code and props to a named remote app through belt. This confirms third-party processing, although the behavior matches the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Required
medium_findings.description: The skill requires Bash access for belt commands and documents npx installation commands for related skills. The commands are transparent and relevant, but they depend on third-party CLI behavior and local authentication state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly allows Bash(belt *) and shows belt and npx command usage. I found no evidence that arguments are hidden or malicious.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Contextual
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh, cloud.inference.sh, Remotion documentation, and related platform documentation. They appear to be documentation or service references, not credential exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links and align with the skill purpose. Network risk remains because rendering depends on the external inference.sh service.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography on metadata and Markdown table lines. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, encryption routines, or cryptographic decision logic in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain description text and a Markdown table header, not crypto code. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill centers on belt app run invocations that execute a remote rendering workflow. Users should review inputs before sending code or data to the service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly invoke a remote app through the CLI. The pattern is risky because it transfers render payloads outside the local environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied TSX Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The input schema requires a code string containing React component TSX. Code payloads can include proprietary logic or sensitive literals if users are not careful.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The schema and import examples confirm that the service consumes code-like input. The concern is data exposure, not confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Network Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on inference.sh URLs and documentation for rendering and setup. This creates availability and privacy dependency on an external provider.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external service dependency is explicit in the documentation. I found no evidence that the URLs are disguised or unrelated to rendering.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from React Code
content.value_statement: Rendering animated React scenes into video often requires setup, encoding, and infrastructure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to use inference.sh for Remotion MP4 renders.
actual_capabilities: Runs Remotion render jobs through the inference.sh belt CLI.
Accepts TSX component code that exports a default React component.
Supports common Remotion APIs such as useCurrentFrame, interpolate, spring, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img.
Documents render settings for width, height, frames per second, duration, codec, composition ID, and props.
Shows Python SDK usage for batch rendering and streaming progress updates.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends render code, props, and related data to an external service.
Does not provide a local Remotion renderer or local sandbox configuration.
Render success depends on supported imports and the remote service environment.

use_cases.title: Prototype Motion Graphics
use_cases.description: Create short animated text, shapes, and transitions from React component ideas.

use_cases.title: Generate Data Videos
use_cases.description: Render counters, dashboard summaries, and data-driven visual updates as shareable MP4 files.

use_cases.title: Automate Video Production
use_cases.description: Use scripted renders and streaming progress to produce repeatable branded videos.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Intro Render
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a short text animation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a three-second Remotion intro with centered text, a clean fade-in, 1080p resolution, and a dark background.

prompt_templates.title: Social Clip Render
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a formatted marketing clip.
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a square Remotion video for a product announcement with headline text, subtitle text, smooth sequencing, and brand colors.

prompt_templates.title: Data Animation Render
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a data-driven video.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion scene that animates three metric values over five seconds and renders it as an MP4 through inference.sh.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Render Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for repeatable production rendering.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that renders multiple Remotion videos with different props and reports progress until each MP4 is available.

output_examples.input: A request for a two-second welcome animation with large centered text.
output_examples.output: A rendered MP4 link and a short summary of the resolution, duration, frame rate, and animation behavior.

output_examples.input: A request to animate a loading counter from zero to one hundred percent.
output_examples.output: A square MP4 render with the counter animation and notes about duration, frame rate, and visual styling.

output_examples.input: A request to render a branded video using supplied title and subtitle values.
output_examples.output: A video result produced from a Remotion component with props applied to the visible text.

best_practices: Remove secrets, private source code, and sensitive customer data before sending render inputs.
Keep initial renders short and low resolution before increasing duration or output size.
Validate Remotion imports and props before starting longer render jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys, tokens, credentials, or proprietary logic into render code.
Do not assume the remote renderer has access to every local file or package.
Do not start long production renders before testing timing, layout, and asset availability.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion component code into MP4 video through the inference.sh service.

faq.question: Does it run completely locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the belt CLI to send render jobs to an external inference.sh app.

faq.question: What inputs can I configure?
faq.answer: You can configure component code, props, composition ID, width, height, frame rate, duration, and codec.

faq.question: Can I use Remotion animation helpers?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img.

faq.question: What should I avoid sending?
faq.answer: Avoid secrets, private source code, customer data, and any asset URL that should not be processed externally.

faq.question: Does it support progress updates?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes Python SDK examples for streaming render progress and reading the final video output.
